channel.title: The Grant Desk - Cross-disciplinary & Social Impact, US
channel.description: Paid open calls, fellowships and residencies in AI, tech, research, and digital and mixed-media arts, sorted into the right pile. Updated as new calls land on the desk. Filtered to: Cross-disciplinary & Social Impact, US.

item.title: OUTsider Fest 2027: Dreaming US into Being (call for queer time-based art)
item.description:
Organisation: OUTsider

Location: Austin, Texas, United States (festival February 2027). International-friendly; prioritizes marginalized artists including by migratory status and nationality.

Award: Festival presentation slot for time-based work (film, performance, sound, multimedia). Artist compensation and travel support not stated. No fee stated.

Duration: Festival February 2027.

Deadline: 20 Jul 2026

OUTsider, the transdisciplinary queer arts festival in Austin, invites LGBTQ+ artists working in any time-based form (film, performance, sound, multimedia) to propose work for its 2027 edition themed Dreaming US into Being. The call prioritizes marginalized artists, explicitly including by migratory status and nationality. Deadline 20 July 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Ucross General Residency (All Disciplines): Spring 2027
item.description:
Organisation: Ucross Foundation

Location: Sheridan, Wyoming, USA

Award: No charge for the residency; living accommodations, meals, and work space provided. USD 40 application fee.

Duration: Spring session (February through early June 2027); residencies range from two to six weeks

Deadline: Closed 15 Jul 2026

Ucross offers fully funded artist residencies on a working cattle ranch in the foothills of Wyoming's Bighorn Mountains, providing uninterrupted time and space free from the pressures and distractions of daily life. Founded in 1981 with its first residency period in 1983, Ucross hosts approximately 115 individuals per year. Residencies range from two to six weeks; at any one time up to ten people are in residence, typically four writers, four visual artists, and two composers. Each artist receives living accommodations, meals, and work space at no charge. Lunches and dinners are prepared Monday to Friday by a professional chef (lunches delivered to studios, group dinners at 6 p.m.), with ample provisions for breakfasts and weekends; towels, linens, and weekly housekeeping are provided, and there is cell service and wireless internet throughout. Residents provide their own working materials and arrange their own travel to Sheridan, Wyoming. The program is open to visual artists, writers, composers, choreographers, interdisciplinary artists, performance artists, and collaborative teams; applicants must exhibit professional standing in their field, and both established and emerging artists are encouraged to apply. Applications are submitted by discipline category: dance, music/sound, performance, film/video, playwriting/screenwriting, visual art, fiction, nonfiction, poetry, or interdisciplinary. The work sample and project proposal are the most significant features of the application and should correspond to one another; if your work is in a single discipline, select the category that best fits your project, and apply under interdisciplinary only if the project genuinely combines multiple disciplines. Work-sample requirements by category: DANCE, 2-3 video files (each up to 8 minutes and 200 MB, or up to 3 Vimeo links, with a timestamp if longer than 8 minutes); MUSIC/SOUND, 3-5 audio files (each up to 8 minutes and 200 MB, or up to 3 Vimeo links; musical scores welcome); PERFORMANCE and FILM/VIDEO, 1-3 video files (each up to 5 minutes and under 200 MB, or up to 3 Vimeo/YouTube links, with a timestamp if longer than 5 minutes); PLAYWRITING/SCREENWRITING, one complete play double-spaced noting the 20-page section to read; VISUAL ART, 10 images at 4x6 inches and 300 dpi (JPG or TIF, no PDFs) plus a data sheet listing title, medium, size, and year; FICTION and NONFICTION, 20 pages of double-spaced writing; POETRY, 6-10 poems (10 maximum) in one PDF, each poem starting on a new page, total not exceeding 15 pages; INTERDISCIPLINARY, samples representing the genres you plan to work in, combining any of the above. All written work samples must be in English. There is a USD 40 application fee. The open call for the Spring 2027 session is open May 1 to July 15, 2026 (apply by 11:59 p.m. MT on July 15). Dedicated Fellowships for Native American Visual Artists and Writers and a separate Culinary Residency are also offered.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Kaatsbaan Subsidized Residencies
item.description:
Organisation: Kaatsbaan Cultural Park

Location: Tivoli, New York, USA

Award: Generously subsidized residency; includes hotel-style on-campus rooms, locally sourced meals, and access to studios, a 160-seat black box theater, and a 153-acre Hudson Valley campus. No artist stipend.

Duration: Individually designed, typically 5 to 21 days

Deadline: Rolling / undated

Kaatsbaan Cultural Park offers subsidized residencies for artists of all genres to create, live, and collaborate on its 153-acre campus in the Hudson Valley near Tivoli, New York. Residencies are individually designed between the artist, Kaatsbaan, and partners to fit the needs of the artist or company, welcoming disciplines from dance, theater, music, and visual arts to writing, composition, design, and film. The program is generously subsidized and includes individual hotel-style rooms, healthy locally sourced meals, and access to professional studios with sprung floors and pianos, a 160-seat black box theater, and exhibition space. Applications are accepted on a rolling basis through an inquiry form, with early inquiries encouraged to secure preferred dates. Artists at any career stage worldwide may apply, individually or as collaborative teams. There is no application fee and no artist stipend.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: ACA Artist Response Fund (ARF) Emergency Relief Grant
item.description:
Organisation: Artist Communities Alliance

Location: United States (ACA member residencies)

Award: Up to USD 1,500

Duration: One-time emergency grant; applications processed within about one week

Deadline: Rolling / undated

The Artist Response Fund (ARF) is an emergency relief program from the Artist Communities Alliance (ACA) for artists who experience threats to their safety or mental health during a residency and must leave before completing their term. Qualifying situations include assault, harassment, or systemic harm where the artist has attempted to address the issue without success. Grants of up to USD 1,500 help cover relocation costs such as transportation home, temporary lodging, supplies or basic needs, and rent upon returning. Eligible applicants are individual artists currently at, or having attended within the past 12 months, an ACA Organizational Member residency. There is no application fee, and the fund operates on a rolling basis with applications typically processed within one week. Artists apply directly through an online form and can also contact ACA staff with questions.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Musicians Foundation Grants
item.description:
Organisation: Musicians Foundation

Location: United States (remote application)

Award: One-time emergency financial assistance; grant amount varies by need. Funds are issued as vendorized checks paid directly to billers (medical providers, landlords, utilities).

Duration: One-time grant

Deadline: Rolling / undated

The Musicians Foundation provides one-time financial assistance to professional musicians facing a specific time of need caused by an unexpected hardship such as a medical or dental situation, a natural disaster, or a housing crisis. Eligible applicants must be professional performers, composers/arrangers, or educators who have worked as professional musicians in the United States for five or more years, currently reside in the U.S., and have filed taxes as a musician for at least five years. The application has a rolling, always-open structure with a Pre-Application followed by a more detailed second part for those who qualify. Grants are issued as vendorized checks made payable directly to the biller and mailed to the applicant's home address. Processing typically takes between one and twelve weeks depending on volume.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Helene Wurlitzer Foundation Residency
item.description:
Organisation: The Helene Wurlitzer Foundation of New Mexico

Location: Taos, New Mexico, USA

Award: Rent-free, utility-paid furnished housing (casita); no stipend (rare stipend support via the Rhonda Artist Stipend Fund); artists cover food and travel USD 30 application fee

Duration: 10 to 12 weeks (Sessions 1 and 3 are 12 weeks, Session 2 is 10 weeks)

Deadline: 18 Jan 2027

The Helene Wurlitzer Foundation of New Mexico runs one of the oldest artist residency programs in the United States, established in 1954, on a 15-acre campus in Taos, New Mexico. The residency is open to visual artists, literary artists, and music composers, including painters, sculptors, photographers, filmmakers, writers, poets, playwrights, screenwriters, and songwriters. Selected grantees receive rent-free and utility-paid housing in a furnished casita for a session of 10 to 12 weeks, with access to a Commons House offering WiFi, a library, and bicycles. No stipend is provided in most cases, and residents cover their own food and transportation. Applications are open to creative artists worldwide regardless of age, experience, or nationality, and the annual deadline is January 18 for residencies the following calendar year. There is a non-waivable USD 30 application fee.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Open Interval: An Artist-Scientist Exploration of Turbulence
item.description:
Organisation: Simons Foundation

Location: United States (trio must be regionally co-located)

Award: USD 30,000 per awarded trio

Duration: April to December 2027 (several months of collaboration)

Deadline: 21 Oct 2026

Open Interval is a Simons Foundation program that supports trios consisting of an artist, a scientist, and a representative from a 501(c)(3) arts organization to collaborate around a shared scientific theme. The current cycle centers on the theme of turbulence, a phenomenon at the edge of chaos and beauty. Awarded trios receive USD 30,000 to support a sustained period of thought partnership and exploration rather than the production of finished works or exhibitions. The program runs from April to December 2027 with a touchstone meeting in May 2027 and possible cohort convenings. Trio members must be regionally co-located anywhere in the United States so they can collaborate in person. The scientist should hold a Ph.D. and be conducting basic research, with priority given to fields such as astrophysics, neuroscience, quantum physics, biology, or mathematics. The application portal opens September 9, 2026, and all three trio members must submit for the application to be complete.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Jentel Artist Residency
item.description:
Organisation: Jentel Foundation

Location: Banner, Wyoming, USA

Award: USD 100 weekly stipend (about USD 300 total over three weeks), plus private bedroom, separate studio space, and shared living and kitchen facilities provided at no cost USD 30 application fee

Duration: Approximately 3 weeks (arrive on the 15th, depart the morning of the 7th of the following month)

Deadline: 15 Sep 2026

The Jentel Artist Residency offers approximately three-week residencies in the foothills of the Bighorn Mountains near Banner, Wyoming, giving artists, writers, and composers dedicated time for creative work. Sessions run from mid-January through early December, hosting six residents at a time. It is open to visual artists in any medium, writers of fiction, creative nonfiction, or poetry, and composers across musical genres. Residents receive a private bedroom, a separate studio, shared living and kitchen space, and a USD 100 weekly stipend to help cover food and personal expenses. Applicants must be at least 25 years old and be U.S. citizens or foreign nationals whose primary residence is in the U.S. during the application year, and may not be enrolled full-time in a degree program. There is a USD 30 non-refundable application fee, with deadlines of September 15 for Winter and Spring sessions and January 15 for Summer and Fall sessions.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: PLAYA Art/Sci Awarded Residency 2027
item.description:
Organisation: PLAYA

Location: Summer Lake, Lake County, Oregon, USA

Award: Awarded residency providing free lodging in a private cabin with kitchen and bathroom plus one weekly group meal. Residents cover their own travel, groceries, and materials. No cash stipend. USD 40 application fee (waivers granted on request by June 28, 2026)

Duration: 11 to 25 nights depending on session

Deadline: Closed 30 Jun 2026

PLAYA's Art/Sci Awarded Residency brings together artists and scientists for immersive, interdisciplinary inquiry at a rural retreat near Summer Lake in Lake County, Oregon. The 2027 cycle offers four session options ranging from 11 to 25 nights between January and November 2027. Awarded residents receive free lodging in a private cabin with kitchen and bathroom plus one weekly group meal, while covering their own travel, food, and materials. Both established and emerging practitioners in any media may apply, including naturalists, biologists, sound artists, new media artists, writers, performers, and interdisciplinary creators, and all applicants must be at least 18 years old. Applications are submitted through SlideRoom and require work samples and two references. There is a USD 40 application fee, though PLAYA states all fee waiver requests will be granted if submitted by June 28, 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Letters to Strangers Mental Health Scholarship 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Letters to Strangers

Location: Open to students and mental health advocates passionate about mental health awareness, advocacy, and creating positive change in their communities.

Award: Up to USD 8,000 toward education.

Duration: One-off scholarship.

Deadline: Closed 30 Jun 2026

The Letters to Strangers Mental Health Scholarship supports students and mental health advocates passionate about mental health awareness, advocacy, and creating positive change in their communities. The award is worth up to USD 8,000 toward education. Apply via the Google Form linked below. Deadline 30 June 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: NEW INC Year 13 Mentor Open Call
item.description:
Organisation: NEW INC (New Museum Incubator)

Location: New York, NY, USA; primarily in-person at 250 Hudson St, lower Manhattan. Some hybrid participation possible.

Award: Dedicated Mentors: $500 honorarium. Office Hours Mentors: $300 honorarium. All mentors also receive a one-year All Access Plus membership to the New Museum. No application fee.

Duration: Year 13 program (September 2026 onward); average 4-8 hours of engagement per week.

Deadline: Closed 08 Jul 2026

NEW INC, the New Museum's cultural incubator, is seeking mentors for Year 13 of its program supporting creative practitioners at the intersection of art, design, and technology. This is a call for mentors (creative and business leaders), not for program members. TWO MENTOR TYPES: Dedicated Mentors ($500 honorarium) are matched one-on-one with a member and commit to ongoing support throughout the year; Office Hours Mentors ($300 honorarium) offer one-on-one sessions to the broader community on an as-needed basis. All mentors receive a one-year All Access Plus membership to the New Museum, invitations to special events including the annual DEMO Festival, and access to community-only workshops, talks, and social events. YEAR 13 PROGRAM TRACKS mentors may support: Art & Code; Creative Science; Extended Realities; Launchpad; Social Architecture. KEY DATES: Mentor Open Call closes 8 July 2026; Mentor Open House (virtual info session) 17 June 2026, 12-1pm ET (register via the site); mandatory Dedicated Mentor matchmaking ranking session 11 September 2026; mandatory Dedicated Mentor matchmaking event 18 September 2026; Dedicated Mentor matches announced 2 October 2026. Most NEW INC activities take place Monday-Friday, 10am-6pm ET. Apply at https://www.newmuseum.org/new-inc/open-call/.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Fulbright-Iméra Mediterranean Studies Award 2027-2028 (Aix-Marseille, US Faculty)
item.description:
Organisation: Iméra – Aix Marseille Université, in partnership with the Fulbright Commission

Location: Aix-Marseille, France (campus residency)

Award: €2,400 (~USD 2,600) monthly stipend; fully covered accommodation (valued USD 788–1,966/month); €1,200 (~USD 1,300) travel allowance. No application fee.

Duration: 5 or 10 months (September 2027 – June 2028, flexible start/end dates).

Deadline: 15 Sep 2026

The Fulbright-Iméra Mediterranean Studies Award supports US faculty to conduct research at the Iméra Institute for Advanced Study at Aix Marseille Université. ELIGIBILITY: mid-career, senior, or community college faculty affiliated with a US institution of higher education; US citizens residing outside France (permanent residents of France are ineligible); any discipline relating to Mediterranean studies; English sufficient (French desirable). 1 or 2 grants awarded. PROVIDED: €2,400/month stipend; fully equipped apartment with linens; office in co-working space; visa fees waived; academic event organisation expenses covered; school placement assistance for dependents. DURATION: 5 or 10 months (September 2027 – June 2028, flexible). DEADLINE: 15 September 2026. Apply via the Fulbright Scholar Program at https://fulbrightscholars.org/award/fulbright-imera-mediterranean-studies-award-aix-marseille-university.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Harvard Radcliffe Institute: Fellowship Program 2027-2028
item.description:
Organisation: Harvard Radcliffe Institute

Location: Cambridge, Massachusetts, USA; fellows must relocate to and reside in the Cambridge/Boston area for the full fellowship (September through May).

Award: USD 78,000 stipend plus an additional USD 5,000 for project expenses. Relocation, housing, and childcare funds may also be provided. No application fee.

Duration: One academic year, September 2027 through May 2028 (residential)

Deadline: 10 Sep 2026

The Harvard Radcliffe Institute Fellowship offers scientists, writers, scholars, public intellectuals, and artists a year to pursue ambitious projects in a vibrant interdisciplinary setting amid the resources of Harvard University. The Institute welcomes innovative work that confronts pressing social, scientific, and policy issues and seeks to engage audiences beyond academia. Reflecting Radcliffe's history, it welcomes (but does not limit eligibility to) proposals focused on women, gender, and society, or that draw on the Schlesinger Library's collections. It also invites proposals relevant to the Institute's 2024-2029 focus area, academic freedom and connecting across difference (intellectual virtues, free and open inquiry, diversity of thought, political polarization, peace and conflict, inequality, religious pluralism, and related higher-education policy issues), including work that constructively challenges disciplinary orthodoxies or advances transformative perspectives. SUPPORT: USD 78,000 stipend plus USD 5,000 for project expenses, with relocation, housing, and childcare funds available; fellows must reside in the Cambridge/Boston area from September through May. ELIGIBILITY: open to individuals across career stages; tenure is not required and applicants need not be academics. This is NOT a postdoctoral fellowship; those currently enrolled in a degree program are ineligible, as are former Harvard Radcliffe fellows (1999-present). Applicants must meet discipline-specific criteria. APPLY: register on the online portal and select an area (Humanities and Social Sciences; Creative Arts; Nonfiction and Journalism; or Science, Engineering, and Mathematics). Materials: application form, CV, 1,400-word project proposal (with bibliography when appropriate), a writing or work sample, and three references. DEADLINES: humanities, social sciences, creative arts, and nonfiction and journalism by 10 September 2026, 5pm ET; science, engineering, and mathematics by 1 October 2026, 5pm ET.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Queer|Art|Mentorship (QAM) 2027 Cycle
item.description:
Organisation: Queer|Art

Location: United States, including US territories; applicants must be self-identified LGBTQ+ artists not currently enrolled in school.

Award: USD 2,500 stipend, plus travel support for the in-person weeklong QAM Fellows Retreat (Spring 2027), 1:1 coaching from Queer|Art staff, outreach to professional connections, and inclusion in the QAM alumni network. No application fee.

Duration: 10 months, January to October 2027

Deadline: Closed 15 Jun 2026

Queer|Art|Mentorship (QAM) develops an intergenerational and interdisciplinary network of support and shared knowledge for LGBTQ+ artists, nurturing exchange between artists at all career levels and working against social separation between generations and disciplines. The 10-month program (January to October 2027) pairs each Fellow with a Mentor in their field. Applicants in Film, Literature, Performance, or Visual Art apply with a specific project they want to develop and select a Mentor they would like to work with; the relationship is driven by monthly 1:1 meetings, monthly virtual group meetings across disciplines, and an in-person weeklong retreat in spring. Fellows also take part in virtual QAM Intros artist talks and the in-person Works-in-Progress (WIP) series. SUPPORT: a USD 2,500 stipend, travel support for the Spring 2027 retreat, 1:1 coaching from Queer|Art staff, outreach to professional connections, and inclusion in the QAM alumni network. ELIGIBILITY: artists working at a generative level in at least one of Film, Literature, Performance, or Visual Art who are self-identified as queer, lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, non-binary, and/or intersex; based in the United States, including US territories; early-career and professionally focused with a body of work already behind them; not currently enrolled in school or university; and with a specific project to work on with a Mentor. This year the program accepts 12 Fellows (3 per field); each Mentor chooses the Fellow they will work with. PROCESS: a two-part application. Part 1 (a brief application) is due 15 June 2026; a smaller group meeting program qualifications is then invited to complete the full application on Slideroom, due 31 July 2026. Mentors and staff review applications in September and all applicants are notified by mid-October 2026; the cycle begins January 2027.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: United We Om: Karma Project Grants (Fall 2026)
item.description:
Organisation: United We Om

Location: United States. Applicants and their service work must be based in the USA

Award: Micro-grants of USD 500 to 2,500 (line-item budget required). Service compensation capped at USD 50 per hour.

Duration: Grants must be completed within six months of receiving funding; awarded September 2026

Deadline: Closed 30 Jun 2026

United We Om's Karma Projects micro-grants support and amplify the good works of people dedicated to karma yoga, acts of selfless service without attachment to outcomes, that create lasting change for individuals, communities, and humanity. The Fund seeks to support individuals and small nonprofits creating change in their communities, using their own life experiences to serve others facing similar struggles, where USD 2,500 would significantly benefit the work and represents 1% or more of an annual budget (nonprofits with annual budgets of USD 250,000 or less). Preference is given to those performing service work without financial compensation. The Fund supports fully formed ideas and projects with specific goals and outcomes: expansions of existing programs, innovative pilots, and projects providing necessary resources to people without access, that can be sustained beyond the grant period without further United We Om funding. NOT FUNDED: projects that are not karma yoga; wellness services to communities with traditional access; inflated or above-market budgets; art, film, music, or theatre performances that do not directly serve an underserved community; organizations with annual budgets over USD 250,000; programs requiring membership or ongoing participant fees; one-day events; partially funded projects (additional funds must already be in place); applicants or service work outside the USA; for-profit businesses; projects led by people not from the community they serve; projects with any participation fee including by-donation; religious activities (mantra and prayer); therapy or medical/herbal advice without an active license; political or societal activism; and projects still in development. Grant funds may not be used for staff salaries, travel, administrative or grant-prep work, legal or accounting services, volunteer stipends, tuition or fee subsidies, software/website/tech, or attendance incentives. Grantees may pay themselves up to USD 50 per hour for active service time. APPLICATIONS: two windows annually. The Fall 2026 window is open and closes 30 June 2026 at midnight; grants awarded September 2026. Separate applications exist for individuals and for nonprofits. Do not use AI to write the application. Questions: Executive Director Matt Jared at matt@unitedweom.org.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Grand Canyon Conservancy Residency 2027 (Artist / Astronomer / Environmental Educator)
item.description:
Organisation: Grand Canyon Conservancy (Grand Canyon National Park)

Location: Grand Canyon National Park, Arizona, United States

Award: Free on-site accommodation in the park, financial support (honorarium/stipend; amount varies by track and session - confirm on the track-specific page), plus marketing/exposure through the Conservancy and the National Park. Alumni network access. No application fee. Three tracks (Artist / Astronomer / Environmental Educator) - apply to one track per cycle.

Duration: Place-based residency at Grand Canyon National Park during 2027; dates and length vary by track; residents are expected to engage the public through programs, workshops, talks or similar

Deadline: Closed 15 Jul 2026

Grand Canyon Conservancy's award-winning residency program invites artists, scientists, historians and educators to live on site at Grand Canyon National Park, pursue place-based research, and engage the public through meaningful programs that deepen understanding of the park's environmental, spiritual and cultural impact. Founded in 2020; competitive application + peer-panel review. THREE TRACKS: (1) Artist in Residence - for contemporary artists making interactive (immersive and/or participatory) work that shapes how people experience place; (2) Astronomer in Residence - for astronomers from any discipline who wish to share their knowledge and enthusiasm for dark skies; (3) Environmental Educator in Residence - for educators who use unique and inspiring methods to pique curiosity, prompt exploration, and build knowledge of the world. Residents receive free accommodation, financial support, and marketing/exposure; alumni return as part of a nationwide network. Read the individual track pages (linked from the main residency page) for track-specific eligibility, session lengths, stipend amounts, public-engagement expectations and any region/citizenship requirements before applying. APPLICATIONS OPEN: 15 May - 15 July 2026 for the 2027 cycle.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Storefront for Art and Architecture: Homelands Open Call (research proposals)
item.description:
Organisation: Storefront for Art and Architecture (with Inventory Press)

Location: New York, NY, United States (symposium at Storefront early 2027)

Award: $5,000 artist fee for the selected proposal, plus curatorial and editorial assistance from Storefront. No application fee.

Duration: Research and development through early 2027 (symposium presentation; print publication consideration in the Homelands Reader)

Deadline: Closed 11 Jun 2026

Open call from Storefront for Art and Architecture (NYC), part of its Homelands long-term research cycle on the political dimensions of memory and how artistic practices make and remake connections to place. Invites artists, architects, researchers, filmmakers, scholars and multidisciplinary practitioners to submit a research proposal (in-progress work or new proposals) that will be presented at the Homelands symposium at Storefront in early 2027, and considered for print publication in the Homelands Reader (co-published with Inventory Press). Diverse forms welcome including video, performance, installation, architectural ideas, writing and other media; cross-disciplinary collaborations encouraged. SUPPORT: $5,000 artist fee plus curatorial and editorial assistance. SELECTION: anonymised jury review by curators, scholars and cultural practitioners; evaluated on the strength and originality of the ideas, depth of engagement, clarity of writing, and alignment with Storefront's mission. Deadline 11 June 2026, 11:59pm EDT.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Centrum: 2027 Residency Application (Self-Directed, Emerging Artist, In the Making)
item.description:
Organisation: Centrum

Location: Fort Worden, Port Townsend, Washington, United States

Award: Residency offering dedicated time, workspace and accommodation at Fort Worden. Financial terms (any stipend or cost to the artist) are not specified at this application stage. No application fee.

Duration: 2027 residency season (dates vary by program)

Deadline: Closed 30 Jun 2026

Centrum's single application covers three 2027 residency programs at Fort Worden in Port Townsend, Washington: Self-Directed Residencies (approx. 55-60 spots), In the Making Residencies (3-5 spots), and the Emerging Artist Residency (6 spots). The program gives artists, writers and creatives across all disciplines time and space to relax, focus and reinvent their practice, alongside a community of other residents. Open to practices including but not limited to visual arts, writing, curatorial, performance, dance, music and social practice. Applicants submit work samples/portfolio, the workspace/accommodation they need, and a short paragraph on what a residency would mean to them, and indicate which program(s) they want to be considered for. Selection is by a panel of alumni jurors; no feedback is offered. ELIGIBILITY: Self-Directed and In the Making are open broadly; the Emerging Artist Residency is restricted to Pacific Northwest-based artists residing in Washington, Oregon, Idaho, Montana or British Columbia. Application window 15 April - 30 June 2026; applications absolutely cannot be accepted after 30 June 2026, so apply early.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Pulitzer Center: U.S. Civil Society Microgrants 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Pulitzer Center

Location: United States

Award: US$2,000 to US$4,000 (draft budget required)

Duration: Up to six months (late June to early December 2026); project closure and impact reporting by December 21, 2026

Deadline: Closed 08 Jun 2026

Microgrant supporting U.S. civil society organizations and groups to build civic engagement projects that use Pulitzer Center-supported reporting to amplify the voices of affected communities, foster public debate, and drive meaningful audience engagement. Projects must engage at least one Pulitzer Center story and/or journalist and focus on one of three issue areas: Information and Artificial Intelligence (impact of AI on society, AI governance dialogue); Climate and the Environment (U.S. fisheries and fishing communities, or climate and labor); or Global Health (mental well-being, or connections to Global South reporting). Supported activities include multi-stakeholder dialogues, public forums, community engagement, creative campaigns (videos, infographics), and other innovative formats such as art exhibitions. Out of scope: projects not using Pulitzer Center reporting, direct advocacy or lobbying, partisan campaigns, and for-profit initiatives. ELIGIBILITY: grassroots organizations, academics and researchers, education institutions, civil society organizations and coalitions, youth movements, and other groups with past experience implementing activities in the United States, a track record of collaboration, and proven experience on the identified topics. APPLICATION: project description (max 400 words), target audience and projected reach, methodology, implementation timeline, preliminary budget, content production and distribution plan if applicable, lead applicant CV plus a letter of recommendation. Selection involves shortlisting and an interview with the lead applicant. A required onboarding meeting for grantees is Wednesday, June 24, 2026 (7:00-8:30pm EDT).

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: New Profit: Catalyze Connected Futures Cohort 2026 (Discovery Form)
item.description:
Organisation: New Profit

Location: United States and its territories (organisation must operate in and primarily serve US communities; cohort convenings held in cities across the United States)

Award: One-year $100,000 unrestricted grant to the organisation, plus a $10,000 leadership development stipend for the Social Entrepreneur, plus strategic advisory support and capacity-building. New Profit covers travel, accommodation and meals for the three in-person convenings.

Duration: 12-month cohort experience. Programming requires 6-8 hours per month on average, including three in-person convenings (3-4 days each), three 2-3 hour virtual learning sessions, and a minimum of six 1:1 strategic advising sessions.

Deadline: Closed 26 May 2026

New Catalyze cohort from venture philanthropy organisation New Profit, supporting US-based non-profits whose core work is bridging active divides (political, racial, ethnic, economic, religious, geographic, generational, or ideological) to enable collective problem-solving in education, economic mobility, democracy, and/or health. Funded organisations must use one or more of: building skills for productive dialogue, conflict, and civic participation; cultivating relationships rooted in trust, understanding, belonging, and mutual accountability; or creating structures for collective action, co-design, problem-solving, or shared decision-making. Organisations receive a one-year $100,000 unrestricted grant, a $10,000 leadership development stipend, and a year of cohort-based capacity building (three in-person convenings, three virtual learning sessions, 1:1 strategic advising). Eligibility: US-focused; independent 501(c)(3) status (own or via fiscal sponsor); annual expenses $250,000-$2 million in most recently closed fiscal year; led by at least one full-time (~30 hrs/week) Social Entrepreneur (co-leadership models considered); core programme/impact approach in operation for at least two years (legal status timeline less important); not a previous New Profit Build or Catalyze grantee. The first step is a Discovery Form (NOT a full application). To be considered for this cycle, complete the Discovery Form by Tuesday 26 May 2026, 2pm PT / 5pm ET, selecting 'Cycle-specific submission' and 'Connected Futures 2026'. If you submitted a Discovery Form within the last 12 months, email selection@newprofit.org for a link to update your prior submission. Invitations to complete a full application will be sent in late June. Discovery Forms are also accepted on a rolling basis for grantmaking cycles in 2026 and beyond. Free to apply. Note: this is an organisational grant, not an individual grant.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Impact Award for Native American Art
item.description:
Organisation: Harpo Foundation

Location: USA (open to applicants worldwide)

Award: $25,000 fellowship

Duration: One-time award

Deadline: Closed 27 Apr 2026

Fellowship to amplify the contributions of under-recognised Native American contemporary visual artists.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Knight Cities Challenge 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Knight Foundation

Location: 26 Knight communities, USA

Award: Up to $200,000 per project ($5M total pool)

Duration: 1 year (project completion)

Deadline: Closed 30 Apr 2026

Open call for individuals and organisations across Knight's 26 communities to submit creative ideas for projects that spark change and leave lasting local impact. Project ideas must focus on at least one of Knight's investment areas: strengthening local news and information, creating pathways for economic opportunity, or cultivating connection through arts, culture and shared places. Applications close 30 April 2026 at 11:59 PM ET.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).
